speaker: interviewer
text: It's September 28th, 1995. We're interviewing Helen Rosenblatt, birth name Diamant-- Diament. And the interviewer is Martha Fraser. We're in New York in the United States, and the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were talking about Crystal Night, November 9th, 1939. The synagogues in your town were burned. Did anything else occur that night?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lot of rabbis were killed defending the Torahs. We heard stories about kicking the rabbis and killing them. But Jews prayed privately in a private apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Following that, were further restrictions placed on Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, new restrictions that people have to leave their homes and be assigned to very cramped quarters. This we called the-- the big ghetto. The big ghetto. We were lucky at this time because we remained still in our apartment. However, the three rooms were shared with three other-- with two other families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were moved in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Moved in from a part where Jews could not go in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this area surrounded by any walls or any wire? Or was it an open area?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. There was a curfew. Nobody would dare to go there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The same, we didn't dare to wear jewelry or fur coats, because it was an order of the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you assigned to any other job at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in this-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in the stat commandant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cleaning.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cleaning his apartment. And then, with escort, I was brought back to my place, where the Jews were allowed to stay, because he lived in the best part of the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And following that, what-- what occurred?

speaker: interviewee
text: It happened that my young brother, who was taken to work, did not return home. We didn't know what happened to him. We heard only from our friends and neighbors, a lot of young people were sent to a labor camp. We didn't hear from him for a long, long time. And one day, a miracle happened. He and the other guys ran off from the camp there. And he returned home. He was bewildered. He didn't know what to talk about. But he was safe because he was with us. And-- and we helped him to come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had he been doing? Had he told you where-- what he had been working on at the labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they were digging some places. He hardly talked about it, because he said, they hit us. They wanted to kill us. This probably was the main job, to survive the killing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, how long did the big ghetto remain in existence?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was until '41, from '39, if I'm not mistaken. Until or before '41. We still were moving around in the big ghetto wearing the signs, the yellow badge, and trying our best to be with our families. We could not buy food, but some farmers smuggled in food. And if you had some money or you had clothing or something, you exchanged for food. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then came the holidays. This was, I think, '41. And this was the beginning of the Final Solution because, as I mentioned, it was planned so thoroughly to hit the Jews where it hurts the most. They picked holidays to make the Final Solution. Not all blocks could be taken care of, so the first Rosh Hashanah, we heard about that some blocks of the big ghetto were already empty, because this was what they did. They sent the Jews off. We still were home, and it was Erev Yom Kippur that we prayed in a private apartment. And I prayed to God. God, I heard already what happened to the other Jews. But save my parents. I will be a very religious person. I will pray. I will do good things. But save my parents. My prayer was not heard, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because when the holiday of Sukkot came on, this was our turn. And this I will never forget. I want also to mention that, at this time, my mother was in a safe bunker in the working place where my brother worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his work? He collected furnitures from the empty Jewish homes. And they had a big place. So one day, he came-- we were still in the big ghetto-- and he put mother into an armoire and brought her over to the bunker where he worked. And nobody knew about it. She was not the only one. A few other mothers were there too.

speaker: interviewee
text: But came the tragic day on Sukkot when we had heard, Alle raus! Alle raus! We left everything what we had, only a little knapsack with a little food, a change of clothes. And here I am. My brother was not there. He was at the working place.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother was safe. My poor father was ahead of me. We formed a line, and he was, at this time, 50-something. He wanted to appear younger. So he pinched his cheek, put on a little hat to look younger, and constantly asked me, don't-- do I look young enough? And I encouraged him. We formed a line to the marketplace-- a single line. And all we had to do to stay online. And when they bark at us the questions, what's your occupation? You had to say something quickly. Quickly, I meant I have to survive. So I am a dressmaker. They probably had use for dressmakers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we came to the big platform, and on the platform, faces that I will never forget. And one finger only made the decision that we prayed on Yom Kippur, where-- who will live and who will die. And here, links, recht. Links means left, and right. My father was ahead of me. He went to one side. I said, quickly, dressmaker-- was sent to the working those. I saw, from far away, him on big trucks loaded. I couldn't hear anything, because women were crying. Children were separated from mothers. It was a terrible scene. And it was soon over. Quietly, we had to form-- those who were on the side for work had to go on a five ahead-- five-deep line, walking. This was a terrible march. We didn't know where we were going, why we were going, and what will be the end of it. But we walked through the Jewish quarter in such an eerie silence there-- a place where little children played, where it's always very noisy-- no one. We walked for a long, long time, not saying a word, until we came to a big factory. This was the place where they sorted us out-- men separate, women on the other side. We were assigned to a big hall in a factory, sitting on a cold floor, waiting for what's going to be the next. They didn't give us food. They didn't talk to us. We didn't talk either. And then a new march-- the march to the small ghetto, to the real ghetto. I have to mention that on our way through the streets, the Poles were lined up on both side, clapping hands and jumping for joy they are getting rid of the Jews. Those were our neighbors. We arrived at the ghetto, and were assigned places where we will live. We came into a small, little house with two rooms only. Once, this was the poorest neighborhood where Jews lived. Once there lived a tailor, and he probably raised family and was happy. But we walked into an empty place-- 10 people. And we were so exhausted, so tired. Everybody looked for a place to sit down. Some three smart girls found a bed. They were fortunate. I found a table where the tailor cut his material, and put my tired body down there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we waited. I couldn't sleep because the table was so cold and so hard. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I was not thinking. I didn't know what will be the next. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Can I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] would you like me to ask how the life in ghetto looked like? Yes, now that everyone's in the small ghetto--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- was your brother in the small ghetto also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. As you mentioned, my brother was assigned to a corner building. And a lot of young people were in this corner building. He brought my mother to the bunker there-- not right away, but after a while. And the boys built a bunker there, underneath of the building. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mothers were there, old mothers. They gave us a little bit of water, and the light came only from a little window because it was in-- in the basement underneath of-- of the street. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the young boys and women who remained had to go to work. Every morning it was dark outside. We heard the bugle. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And now, whenever I hear it, it comes back to me. They called us for work. We have-- we-- we-- we didn't have names anymore. We were assigned to work every group. I was assigned to Jewish homes to collect pots and pans. This was my job. When I came to this apartment, I still found a piece of bread that the people who left in a hurry could not eat, pictures that I could not save any for own my own home. And those pots and pans we had to bring to the market, a big market where the Poles came to buy for a very cheap price. We were assigned to a group. And our supervisor-- his name was Rachner-- he had a vicious dog. He had a face of a tyrant. He watched us. We worked there in the marketplace, selling the pots and pans to the Polish customers, and he collected the money. It was a day in November, '42, I think so. And it was rainy day. The Poles didn't come. No costumers. Rachner didn't get money. He was furious. He looked for a little bit of entertainment. Took out two women from my group, brought them behind the building, and we heard shots. They never came back. He came back to our entire group, took out a gun, and started shooting. I didn't know that he hit me [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in my leg. And I didn't know that the shell is still there. I couldn't walk. But the girls-- when we returned home, the girls holded onto both sides so I can safely come to the ghetto back. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I came back. I couldn't walk. I couldn't stay in my place, because I had to go to work. So my brother carried me to the bunker. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in the bunker was my mother. My mother could take care of me as much as she could, but we didn't have medication. The leg was swollen. I needed help from a doctor. We had a little infirmary in the ghetto, attended by two doctors, Jewish doctors. Their order of the day was that, whenever they have surgery, they have to notify in advance the Haupt commandant of our ghetto so he can attend it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, his name was Degenhart. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was feeling very, very bad there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and worse and worse. Finally, a decision came with my brother at night, with my mother, that he will bring me at night to the doctor. He should see what we can do. He carried me to Dr. Glatter in the infirmary, and he said, the shell is in there. She's going to lose the leg. She has to go through surgery. Tomorrow you bring her in, and you have to follow all the orders. And now we had to make a decision. My mother, with tears in her eyes, said, if we can save your leg, let's go. I was brought to the little hospital, and the only nurse, Helen [? Kamatries-- ?] God save her soul-- she talked to me, you cannot cry. It will hurt. We don't have anesthesia. All I can is put a lot of cotton into your mouth so you will not scream, because this is a pleasure for our commandant, and you can not disturb his pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: He enjoyed watching the surgery?

speaker: interviewee
text: He enjoyed because the Jewish blood in a young person there, not screaming-- he-- he satisfied himself. It was more than a sexual satisfy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I had to remain in this hospital for a few days. I will come back to it. But before this happened, I want to mention to you that working for our oppressors didn't kill my soul and my faith. If I have to die, I wanted to die with honor. I joined the underground in the building where the bunker was. Only young people lived here, and this was the headquarter of the underground Jewish fighters. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They prepared to do what's possible before they die. We worked on making a tunnel, since this was on the-- on-- close to the barbed wire. On the other side were the Poles. We made a tunnel underneath of this building. And then we got the contact with the underground on the Polish side, who helped. They were not all Poles, but they were the Poles who had a feeling for the innocent Jews. And we smuggled out money, whatever we had. We didn't have any money. But since we got from them money, we could buy guns to protect ourselves. And the plan was that, when the day will come, and we have to die, we will die like Samson died, with dignity, shaking the pillars. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In the bunker, we made Molotov cocktail bottles to defend ourselves. This went on until '43, a long time. And then, probably, somebody let the German know about it, what we intend to do. And then it came the last day. But back to my operation, one good thing happened there in the hospital. Dr. Glatter, who operated, was attending to another camp-- labor camp that was far away from ours. And I had a conversation with him, and I mentioned the name of my boyfriend. And I didn't know anything about him, and he said he is alive. This was the good thing. Quickly, I scribbled a few words to him, and then we know already that we were both alive. When will we see each other, we didn't know. It took a long time. And then they sent all the boys from there to our camp, and this was the happy reunion. And shortly after this, we got married. But this was already very close to the end story of the ghetto. It was June, '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: You got married in June '43?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And I was married only for one week, being with him. And then came the final day of the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They asked us to go to the marketplace. Again, a selection. There were children too. And there I had my mother. We made a new bunker for my mother on the-- in the attic of the building where we were assigned with my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time, had they already discovered the underground and the tunnel?

speaker: interviewee
text: They knew already about it. We knew something is going to happen. But it didn't happen yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took out some young people, killed them. Killing was very usual in the ghetto. One day, it was on Purim-- Purim Holiday. They announced that all the professionals in the ghetto will have a chance to go to Palestine. And they came. They were lawyers, teachers. I was a teacher. I didn't have the nerve to go. Since I had the mother, I could  not go. They went. They never came back. They were shot. They dig their own graves and never came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find that that-- out that that was what happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Poles that lived around knew about it. And since they had supplied some food for the ghetto or something, somebody brought the news. And we knew about it. I worked for the underground with all my dedication. Let me mention some little scene. Before it happened with my leg, I was assigned to coming back from the work to smuggle in a gun. It was not so very easy because we were inspected thoroughly at the entrance of the ghetto. Some smuggled food. This was taken away. However, I had to do it. It was my mission. I concealed it. But the gun was so cold. I still feel it. I smuggled it in.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- I was not a hero. I just did it because there was not tomorrow, so what can I lose? At least we can kill a few German. And so did others, too. And we get some guns in the tunnel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when the news came out [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that the Jews are there preparing some revenge, then they took us all out to the market-- a new selection. Now my poor mother is again in a bunker. Before I left my place, I walked up to her. I said, this, what happened the first time will happen now. When everything is over, we take you out from there. Don't worry. I walked already, and then I had the feeling that maybe she's very sad. I walked back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was a very wrong move, because an SS man stopped me on the way. Where are you going? I said, this is the piece of bread that I left on the table. I'm going. He didn't do anything because he probably figured out, early or later, you will come to your destiny. We came to the market.
